items.title: Define the MDP ingredients before coding
items.avoid: Jumping into a library without specifying states, actions, rewards, episode boundaries, and what success means.
items.prefer: Write a one-paragraph MDP spec: what the agent observes, what it can do, how reward is computed, and when episodes reset.
items.why: Ambiguous problem definitions cause silent bugs—wrong reward signs, non-Markov observations, or impossible action spaces.
items.example: CartPole: state = (position, velocity, angle, angular rate); actions = {push left, push right}; reward = +1 per step until failure.

items.title: Start with simulators and baselines
items.avoid: Training on expensive real hardware before a random policy and simple heuristic baseline are measured.
items.prefer: Log random-policy return, then a scripted baseline; only then compare learned policies in the same environment seed.
items.why: Without baselines you cannot tell whether learning improved anything or the environment is trivial/broken.
items.example: If random CartPole averages 22 steps and your agent gets 25, improvement is real but modest—document both numbers.

items.title: Align reward with the true goal
items.avoid: Proxy rewards that are easy to measure but gameable (e.g., button presses instead of task completion).
items.prefer: Reward outcomes you actually want; test for reward hacking in simulation before deployment.
items.why: Agents optimize the reward you give, not the intention behind it—Goodhart's law applies strongly in RL.
items.example: A cleaning robot rewarded for dust sensor readings might learn to cover the sensor instead of cleaning the room.

items.title: Separate evaluation from exploration
items.avoid: Reporting performance while epsilon-greedy exploration is still active during test runs.
items.prefer: Fix or freeze the policy (epsilon = 0, deterministic argmax) for evaluation episodes with held-out seeds.
items.why: Random exploration inflates variance and hides whether the learned policy actually works.
items.example: Evaluate with `epsilon=0` for 100 episodes; report mean and standard deviation of return.
